remission.spontaneous_remission_rate: 60-85% achieve seizure freedom on medication; of those, 50-75% remain seizure-free after withdrawal (varies by syndrome and duration of freedom)
remission.best_intervention_remission_rate: Not clearly higher than on-medication rates -- the real gap is behavioral/practice
remission.gap_size: Small biological gap; large practice gap
remission.barrier_detail: Only 26-45% of seizure-free patients who are candidates for medication withdrawal ever attempt it; physician and patient reluctance vastly exceeds biological risk
remission.last_soc_change: 2023 — FDA approved cenobamate, fenfluramine for Dravet; first-line ASM (levetiracetam, lamotrigine, valproate) unchanged since 1990s-2000s; ketogenic diet formally endorsed in 2018 AES guidelines
remission.outdated_treatments: Phenobarbital as first-line in adults (cognitive effects, dependence; still >50% of first-line therapy in low-income countries and some US rural settings); phenytoin loading in status epilepticus (fosphenytoin preferred); carbamazepine without therapeutic drug monitoring
remission.adjunctive_emerging: Ketogenic diet (proven for drug-resistant epilepsy, underutilized); vagus nerve stimulation; responsive neurostimulation (RNS); low-glycemic index treatment; CBD (Epidiolex approved, broader CBD products under study)
remission.frontier_signal: Gene therapy for SCN1A (Dravet syndrome) in early trials; antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs) for genetic epilepsies; optogenetic approaches; closed-loop neurostimulation with seizure prediction
remission.notes: Manually curated from landmark trials, registries, and guidelines (disease_db_100.json). Protected from automated overwrite.
